name: Pharoah Sanders
abstract: Pharoah Sanders (born Ferrell Lee Sanders; October 13, 1940 – September 24, 2022) was an American jazz saxophonist. Known for his overblowing, harmonic, and multiphonic techniques on the saxophone, as well as his use of "sheets of sound", Sanders played a prominent role in the development of free jazz and spiritual jazz through his work …

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: I had the opportunity to play with him with John Coltrane in Chicago at the Plugged Nickel with Alice and Jimmy and Pharoah Sanders.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, April 7, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Tyner McCoy
about_quotes.evidence: the album, Blues for Coltrane actually got you the, you were awarded the Grammy for best jazz instrumental performance and that album included David Murray, Pharaoh Sanders, Cecil McBee, and Roy Haynes
about_quotes.doc_title: Tyner McCoy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: I did something called “The Reverend King Suite” which was actually written by John Coltrane, on our album called Cosmic Music with Alice and Rashied, Pharaoh Sanders.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mcbee
about_quotes.evidence: So when you played with Alice Coltrane and Pharoah Sanders did you feel comfortable with the intent, the seeming intent of the music?

about_quotes.speaker: Jane Bunnett
about_quotes.evidence: I think like I mean I just feel like I’m influenced by so many things that I just hear, particular pieces like Pharoah Sanders
about_quotes.doc_title: Jane Bunnett interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, January 25, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Randy Weston
about_quotes.evidence: On that date, we also had the great Pharaoh Sanders. With Pharaoh Sanders we did one of the Gnawa pieces, “Blue Moses”
about_quotes.doc_title: Randy Weston — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
